questions.question: Which statement best describes what sepsis is?
options: A type of infection that only affects the lungs
The body's overwhelming and life-threatening response to an infection, which begins to damage its own tissues and organs
A long-term condition that develops slowly over many years
A mild skin rash that clears up on its own within a few days

questions.question: Why does speed matter so much with sepsis?
options: It is contagious and spreads quickly between people in a care home
It can develop from a minor infection and become deadly within hours, and every hour of delay increases the risk of harm
It only becomes dangerous after several weeks, so there is time to observe
It always clears up by itself if left alone for a day

questions.question: What is the care worker's role when sepsis is suspected?
options: To start antibiotics and give fluids straight away
To recognise that the person is seriously unwell and escalate immediately
To wait and watch to see whether the person improves
To diagnose the type of infection before telling anyone

questions.question: A person you support suddenly seems very confused and “not themselves”. What is the most helpful thing to do?
options: Assume they are just having an off day and check again tomorrow
Try to work out yourself which infection they have before telling anyone
Ask “could this be sepsis?” and escalate the change straight away
Wait until the end of your shift to mention it in the handover

questions.question: Which infections can lead to sepsis?
options: Only chest infections such as pneumonia
Almost any infection — including chest, urine, wound and skin infections
Only very serious infections that need hospital treatment from the start
Only infections in people who have just had an operation

questions.question: Why is it important to actually say the word “sepsis” when you escalate?
options: It confirms the diagnosis so treatment can begin without a clinician
It signals a possible emergency and prompts others to act urgently
It replaces the need to describe what you have actually seen
It is only needed when writing up notes after the emergency is over

questions.question: Which everyday action helps reduce a person's risk of developing an infection that could lead to sepsis?
options: Following infection control procedures, keeping catheters and wounds clean, and supporting good hydration
Skipping hand hygiene when you are busy to save time
Leaving a catheter drainage system open so it drains more easily
Waiting until a wound looks badly infected before mentioning it

questions.question: Which of these is a common part of recovery after sepsis that you should be aware of?
options: An immediate and complete return to normal within a day of leaving hospital
A reason to stop reporting any new changes in the person's health
A guarantee that the person can never get an infection or sepsis again
Lasting tiredness, low mood and problems with memory, sometimes called post-sepsis syndrome

questions.question: When someone has sepsis, what is it that actually causes the harm to the body?
options: The person's own immune response going into overdrive and injuring their tissues and organs
A lack of vitamins in the person's diet
The antibiotics used to treat the infection
A single germ that attacks only the heart

questions.question: Sepsis is sometimes called “blood poisoning”, but the course explains that it is really:
options: A condition caused only by contaminated food
A poison deliberately introduced into the bloodstream
The body's own reaction to infection that causes the harm
Something that only happens after a blood transfusion

questions.question: Which group is at higher risk of becoming seriously unwell with sepsis?
options: Older and frail people, especially those over 75
Only people who have never had an infection
Only professional athletes
Only people who live alone

questions.question: Why do devices such as a urinary catheter or a drip line add to the risk of sepsis?
options: They are always painful and cause shock
They cause the immune system to switch off completely
They give germs a route inside the body
They make the person's temperature rise on their own

questions.question: Which single question does the course encourage you to ask when someone is suddenly unwell?
options: “Which antibiotic should I give?”
“Can you wait until tomorrow?”
“Is this worth mentioning at all?”
“Could this be sepsis?”

questions.question: A family member tells you a person is “not themselves” and they seem more confused and drowsy than usual. How should you treat this?
options: As a reason to let the person sleep it off before mentioning it
As an important early warning that should be passed on quickly
As nothing to worry about, because moods change
As information only useful for the next annual review

questions.question: Which of the following is listed as a common warning sign of sepsis in adults?
options: Slow, relaxed breathing
Bright, clear, pink skin
New confusion or slurred speech
A strong, steady appetite

questions.question: What does the course say about a person's temperature in sepsis?
options: It is always very high with a clear fever
It is always normal, so temperature can be ignored
It may be either very high or abnormally low, and an older person may not develop a high fever at all
It has no connection to sepsis at all

questions.question: How might the skin of someone developing sepsis look?
options: Always covered in a green rash
Mottled, pale, blotchy or bluish, especially on the lips, hands or feet
Deeply tanned and warm all over
Completely unchanged in every case

questions.question: Some people with sepsis describe an overwhelming feeling of impending doom. How should this be treated?
options: Recorded only after the emergency is over
Ignored unless a temperature is also high
Dismissed as ordinary worry
Always taken seriously

questions.question: A person you support has a urinary catheter and their urine has become cloudy and smelly, and they seem more unwell. What should you do?
options: Assume it is normal for anyone with a catheter
Wait a few days to see whether it clears on its own
Report it, staying alert to the possibility of infection and sepsis
Remove the catheter yourself immediately

questions.question: Someone you support is already being treated with antibiotics for an infection but is becoming more unwell rather than better. What does this mean?
options: Sepsis can develop even while treatment is under way, so this is an important warning to escalate
You should stop watching them because treatment is under way
The infection must have gone, so the decline is unrelated
Antibiotics always prevent sepsis, so there is nothing to worry about

questions.question: The most dangerous thing you can do when sepsis is possible is to:
options: Note the time you became concerned
Tell a nurse or senior straight away
Wait and watch to see how the person goes
Say the word “sepsis” out loud

questions.question: When should you call 999 for a person with possible sepsis?
options: Never, because care workers are not allowed to call 999
When the person is very unwell — for example struggling to breathe, hard to rouse, or with mottled or blue skin
Only after waiting a full hour to be sure
Only once a doctor has already confirmed the diagnosis

questions.question: You raised a concern with a nurse, but the person keeps getting worse. What should you do?
options: Wait until your next shift to raise it again
Leave the person alone so they can rest
Speak up again and be prepared to call 999 yourself
Assume it is being dealt with and say nothing more

questions.question: After you have raised the alarm for possible sepsis, what should you do with the person?
options: Give them food and drink to build their strength
Move them to another room and lock the door
Stay with them where you can, keep them comfortable and keep watching for change
Leave them alone to rest until help arrives

questions.question: What information is most useful to note and pass on when you suspect sepsis?
options: Nothing — recording anything just wastes time
Only the person's date of birth
The time you first became concerned, the signs, when they started and any observations taken
A guess about which antibiotic will work best

questions.question: How should recording relate to escalating when sepsis is suspected?
options: Recording matters, but it must never slow down escalation — get help first, then record the detail
Recording should only be done a week later
Recording should always be completed before getting any help
Recording replaces the need to escalate

questions.question: Which of these best supports a person's body in resisting infection in the first place?
options: Avoiding hand hygiene when busy
Keeping them dehydrated and inactive
Skipping skin care to save time
Supporting good hydration, nutrition and movement

questions.question: How should a urinary catheter and its drainage system be kept, in line with the care plan and your training?
options: Clean and closed
Left unchecked for as long as possible
Open and loose so it drains faster
Shared between different people to save equipment

questions.question: Which changes in a wound or pressure area should be reported promptly?
options: Any wound that is fully healed
Increasing redness, swelling, heat, pain or discharge
Only wounds that are completely painless
Only wounds on the person's hands

questions.question: What does the course say about how long recovery from sepsis can take?
options: Everyone recovers fully within one day
Recovery is always instant with no after-effects
Nobody ever recovers from sepsis
Recovery can take weeks or months, and some people are left with lasting effects

questions.question: Which of these is described as a cognitive after-effect that can be part of post-sepsis syndrome?
options: A permanently raised temperature
Improved athletic performance
Problems with memory and concentration
Perfect long-term health

questions.question: How should you support someone recovering after sepsis?
options: Stop monitoring them completely because they have recovered
Encourage rest balanced with gentle activity, support hydration and nutrition, and be understanding about tiredness and low mood
Insist on vigorous exercise straight away regardless of tiredness
Tell them their tiredness and low mood are unusual and their fault

questions.question: Why should you keep watching someone who has already had sepsis?
options: Because sepsis only ever happens once and never returns
Because they can become unwell again with a new infection or further decline
Because they are now immune to all infections
Because they no longer need any care at all

questions.question: What sort of language should you use when recording what you observed?
options: Vague guesses about the cause
Assumptions about what will happen next
Plain, factual and objective language describing what you saw and when
Only medical jargon that others may not understand

questions.question: A rash that does not fade when you press a glass against it is:
options: A separate medical emergency in its own right that means you should not wait
A normal sign that can safely be ignored
Only important in children
A reason to wait several hours before acting

questions.question: How certain do you need to be before you escalate a concern about sepsis?
options: You must have a doctor's confirmation in advance
You must have taken a full set of observations twice
You must be completely certain and rule out every other cause first
You never need to feel certain — if you are worried enough, raise the alarm

questions.question: Which starting-point infection could be easy to overlook in the people you support?
options: A common cough that has fully cleared
A broken bone with no infection
A healed surgical scar from years ago
A urine infection or an infected pressure sore

questions.question: If a false alarm turns out not to be sepsis after you escalated, this is:
options: A serious mistake you should avoid repeating
A reason to stop escalating in future
Something you must apologise for at length
Never a problem — it is exactly what you are meant to do

questions.question: Which organisation is named as a source of further support for people affected by sepsis?
options: The local library
A gardening club
The UK Sepsis Trust
A car insurance company

questions.question: Sepsis is a life-threatening medical emergency that can arise from almost any infection.
options: True
False

questions.question: New confusion or disorientation in someone you support can be an early warning sign of sepsis.
options: True
False

questions.question: If you suspect sepsis, it is best to wait a few hours to see whether the person improves before telling anyone.
options: True
False

questions.question: A care worker's role includes diagnosing sepsis and starting antibiotic treatment.
options: True
False

questions.question: Someone with a normal temperature can still have sepsis.
options: True
False

questions.question: Sepsis can only develop from very serious infections and never from a seemingly minor one.
options: True
False

questions.question: Good hand hygiene and infection control are among the best defences against sepsis, because sepsis starts with infection.
options: True
False

questions.question: Once someone has survived sepsis, they can never become seriously unwell from infection again.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are recognised warning signs of sepsis in adults? (Select all that apply)
options: New confusion or slurred speech
Passing little or no urine
Mottled, pale or bluish skin
Fast breathing or breathlessness
A steady, calm heartbeat and clear pink skin

questions.question: Which groups are at higher risk of becoming seriously unwell with sepsis? (Select all that apply)
options: People with a weakened immune system, for example due to diabetes or chemotherapy
Healthy young adults who have never had any infection
People who have recently had surgery or an invasive procedure
Older and frail people, especially those over 75

questions.question: Which infections can be a starting point for sepsis? (Select all that apply)
options: Chest infections such as pneumonia
Urinary tract infections
Wound, skin and surgical infections
A fully healed scar with no signs of infection

questions.question: When you suspect sepsis, which actions should you take? (Select all that apply)
options: Wait until the end of your shift before mentioning it
Say the word “sepsis” out loud
Call 999 if the person is very unwell
Escalate immediately to a senior or nurse

questions.question: Which everyday actions help prevent the infections that can lead to sepsis? (Select all that apply)
options: Keeping catheters and drainage systems clean and closed
Thorough hand hygiene at the right moments
Reporting changes in wounds and pressure areas promptly
Skipping infection control procedures when short of time

questions.question: Which of these are possible after-effects that can be part of post-sepsis syndrome? (Select all that apply)
options: Problems with memory and concentration
Extreme tiredness and weakness
A guarantee of complete lifelong immunity to infection
Low mood or anxiety

questions.question: Once you have raised the alarm for possible sepsis, what should you do while help is on the way? (Select all that apply)
options: Keep them comfortable and offer calm reassurance
Stay with the person and not leave them alone
Leave to complete your other tasks and return later
Keep watching for any change and report it straight away

questions.question: Sepsis is the body's overwhelming and life-threatening response to an ___.
When you escalate a concern, you should say the word ___ out loud so others act quickly.
If the person is very unwell, call ___ and ask for an ambulance without delay.
The care worker's role is to recognise and ___; it does not include diagnosing or treating.
As soon as you can, note the ___ you first became concerned and what you observed.
